elements.sent-2411-001: Leftist heaps and skew heaps are two well-known data structures for mergeable priority queues.
elements.sent-2411-002: Leftist heaps are constructed for efficiency in the worst-case sense whereas skew heaps are self-adjusting, designed for efficiency in the amortized sense.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we analyze the amortized complexity of leftist heaps to initiate a full performance comparison with skew heaps.
elements.sent-2411-004: We consider both the leftist heaps originally developed by Crane and Knuth, which are also referred to as rank-biased (or, height-biased) leftist heaps, and the weight-biased leftist heaps introduced by Cho and Sahni.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show how weight-biased leftist heaps satisfy the same exact amortized bounds as skew heaps.
elements.sent-2411-006: With these matching bounds we establish a nice trade-off in which storage of weights is used to limit the worst-case complexity of leftist heaps, without affecting the amortized complexity compared to skew heaps.
elements.sent-2411-007: For rank-biased leftist heaps, we obtain the same amortized lower bounds as for skew heaps, but whether these bounds are tight is left as an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-008: It's a true pleasure to dedicate this paper to the celebration of Joost-Pieter Katoen's sixtieth birthday.
elements.sent-2411-009: In 1988 we got to know each other as fresh computer science graduates, sharing office ``Club 737'' at TU Eindhoven with two more students.
elements.sent-2411-010: I have very fond memories of those days.
elements.sent-2411-011: We were hardworking but also had a lot of fun.
elements.sent-2411-012: One thing that springs to mind is how Joost (as I got to know him) would throw his pencil eraser full-force against the blinds, distressing whoever was sitting behind {\em our Olivetti desktop PC facing the window.}
elements.sent-2411-013: And this was not the only way he kept us sharp.
elements.sent-2411-014: Joost did his master's at the University of Twente and together with his no-nonsense attitude this was a welcome change for the rest of us, all from Eindhoven.
elements.sent-2411-015: Our Club 737 participation to the Nuenen relay triathlon with Pieter Struik swimming, Joost cycling, and me running is also memorable, not the least for the announcement of our disqualification even before I was finishing in first position (ever) because Joost had been sent the wrong way, making an early U-turn.
elements.sent-2411-016: He quickly picked up the ``calculational style'' of programming which was pretty much baked into every computer science course at TU Eindhoven those days, since E.W.\ Dijkstra started this in the seventies.
elements.sent-2411-017: We did this not only for imperative programming, using the Guarded Command Language, but also for procedural, functional, concurrent, parallel, and even logical programming.
elements.sent-2411-018: One particular topic we happened to work on together was systolic arrays, which later resulted in two joint papers~\cite{KS91parallel,KS96 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Joost made sure that we got those two papers published, not just leave it as an internal project.
elements.sent-2411-020: I think he pretty much continued that way, working on one project after the other, building this impressive and massive research portfolio we know him for today.
elements.sent-2411-021: Thinking about a topic for the present paper, I recalled seeing their recent paper on amortized {\em expected complexity~\cite{BKK+23 , which combines probabilistic reasoning and amortized analysis in a formal setting.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: This fits nicely with the work by Tobias Nipkow et al.\ (see, e.g.,~\cite{NB19 ) on formal verification of previously published amortized analyses, in the same style as initiated in my PhD thesis~\cite{Sch92 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Another interesting development in this direction is the work by Lorenz Leutgeb et al., continuing a series of papers by the late Martin Hofmann (see~\cite{LMZ21 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-024: In fact, the latter paper presents a slightly improved analysis of splay trees compared to the bounds in~\cite{ST85 : by changing the potential function into $\Phi'{(x) =\Pot{(x) -\log_2\sz{x}$ the amortized costs for the delete operation are reduced from $6\log_2\sz{x}$ to $5\log_2\sz{x}$ comparisons, while retaining the bounds for the other operations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The new bound was discovered by their automated analysis of splaying, which builds on elements of type checking systems, starting from the analysis in~\cite[Section 11.2]{Sch92 and~\cite{Sch93 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In light of the linear constraint systems used in~\cite{LMZ21 we also like to mention the use of linear programming to find (optimal) potential functions by Sleator~\cite{Sle92 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: With all these developments in mind and with the realization that Joost-Pieter is still going strong at contributing to these topics in modern computer science, I'll be happy to conclude this paper with a challenging open problem, conjecturing that the lower bound for rank-biased leftist heaps presented below is in fact tight.
elements.sent-2411-028: Leftist heaps were invented by Crane~\cite{Cra72 and further developed by Knuth, see~\cite[Section~5.2.3]{Knu98 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Apart from supporting the usual priority queue operations such as $\INS{ {}$ and $\DLM{}$, leftist heaps also support $\UNI{ {}$ for merging (or, melding) heaps.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Leftist heaps achieve logarithmic time for all these operations.
elements.sent-2411-031: Nowadays, there exist lots of alternative priority queue implementations, often with better performance, such as merging in constant time (see, e.g., \cite{Bro13 ), but leftist heaps, and skew heaps as their self-adjusting counterparts, are very simple to implement and generally applicable.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Leftist heaps are slightly more complicated than (top-down) skew heaps.
elements.sent-2411-033: Skew heaps are self-adjusting and require no storage other than the storage required for the heap itself.
elements.sent-2411-034: Leftist heaps either store the weight or the rank for each subtree.
elements.sent-2411-035: Weight-biased leftist heaps were introduced by Cho and Sahni~\cite{CS96 as a single (top-down) pass variant of the original rank-biased leftist heaps due to Crane and Knuth.}
elements.sent-2411-036: As highlighted by Okasaki~\cite{Oka99 , the weight-biased variant is also favorable in combination with lazy evaluation; similar advantages apply in combination with concurrent evaluation, as first considered by Jones~\cite{Jon89 .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: As skew heaps keep no additional information at all, the same advantages apply there as well.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \fbox{\begin{tabular}{l} $\begin{array}{lcl} \EMP = \Bin{} \ISE{x} = x=\Bin{} \SIN{a} = \Bin{a} \UNI{x}{y} = \ch{x}{y} \MNM{\Bin{}} = \infty \MNM{\Bin{t,a,u}} = a \DLM{\Bin{t,a,u}} = \ch{t}{u} \end{array}$ \vspace*{1mm} $\begin{array}{lcll} \ch{\Bin{}}{\Bin{}} = \Bin{} \ch{\Bin{t,a,u}}{y} = \bal{\Bin{t,a,\ch{u}{y}}}, a\leq\MNM{y} \ch{x}{\Bin{t,a,u}} = \bal{\Bin{t,a,\ch{x}{u}}}, otherwise \end{array}$ \end{tabular}} \vspace*{3mm} \fbox{$\begin{array}{c@{ }lcll} Skew heaps \bal{\Bin{t,a,u}} = \Bin{u,a,t} [1mm] Weight-biased \bal{\Bin{t,a,u}} = \Bin{t,a,u}, \size{t}>\size{u} leftist heaps \Bar \Bin{u,a,t}, \size{t}\leq\size{u} [1mm] Rank-biased \bal{\Bin{t,a,u}} = \Bin{t,a,u}, \rank{t}>\rank{u} leftist heaps \Bar \Bin{u,a,t}, \rank{t}\leq\rank{u} \end{array}$} \end{center} \caption{Purely functional binary heaps with three balancing strategies.} \label{tdsh}
elements.sent-2411-038: A simple implementation of the three types of heaps is shown in Figure~\ref{tdsh , omitting the redundant operation $\INS{a {x =\UNI{\SIN{a {x}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: We use \Bin{ to denote an empty tree and \Bin{t,a,u to denote a binary tree with left subtree $t$, root value $a$, and right subtree $u$, with \Bin{a as a shorthand for \Bin{\Bin{ ,a,\Bin{ .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The weight \size{x of tree $x$ is defined by $\size{\Bin{ =0$ and $\size{\Bin{t,a,u =\size{t +\size{u +1$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-042: Binary heaps satisfy the heap property implying that the root holds the minimum value for each subtree.
elements.sent-2411-043: In addition, for weight-biased heaps, the weight of each left subtree is at least the weight of its right sibling, and for rank-biased heaps, the rank of each left subtree is at least the rank of its right sibling.
elements.sent-2411-044: Note that weights require about $\log_2 n$ bits of storage for size-$n$ subtrees, while $\log_2 \log_2 n$ bits suffice for ranks, due to (\ref{eq:rank ).}
elements.sent-2411-045: As a suitable cost measure $\T{ {}$ for the time complexity of binary heaps we count the number of comparisons $a\leq\MNM{y}$ used for unfolding $\ch{ {}$, including all comparisons with $\infty$.}}

elements.section-2411-003: Simple Amortized Analysis of Leftist Heaps
elements.sent-2411-055: We perform an amortized analysis in terms of a potential function $\Pot{}$, where the amortized costs are given by: \begin{equation}{lllll} \A{\UNI{}{}}{x,y} = \T{\ch{}{}}{x,y} + \Pot{(\ch{x}{y})} -\Pot{(x)} -\Pot{(y)}, \A{\DLM{}{}}{x} = \T{\ch{}{}}{t,u} + \Pot{(\ch{t}{u})} - \Pot{(x)}, \ \ with x=\Bin{t,a,u}. \end{equation} Taking $\Pot{(x) =\rank{x}$ gives us the following bounds for leftist heaps, using (\ref{eq:rank ): \begin{equation}{lllll} \A{\UNI{}{}}{x,y} = \rank{x}+\rank{y} + \rank{(\ch{x}{y})} - \rank{x} -\rank{y} \leq \log_2 \sz{\ch{x}{y}}, \A{\DLM{}{}}{x} = \rank{t} + \rank{u} + \rank{(\ch{t}{u})} - \rank{u} - 1 \leq 2\,\log_2 \sz{x}. \end{equation} This means that the bound for \UNI{ { is reduced by 50\%, while retaining the bound for \DLM{ .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Note that (\ref{eq:rank ) actually implies the same bounds for any potential function $\Pot{}$ satisfying $\rank{x}\leq\Pot{(x)}\leq\log_2 \sz{x}$.}
elements.section-2411-004: Amortized Analysis of Weight-Biased Leftist Heaps
elements.sent-2411-057: We have reduced the bound for \UNI{ { by 50\% but for many applications the overall cost is dominated by the cost of the \DLM{ operations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Therefore we want to make the bound for \DLM{ as tight as possible.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In this section we will show that for weight-biased leftist heaps the upper bound for \A{\DLM{ {x can be improved to $\log_\phi \sz{x}\approx1.44\,\log_2 \sz{x}$, where $\phi=(\sqrt{5 {+ 1)/2\approx1.618$ is the golden ratio.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The lower bound in Section~\ref{section:lower-bound will imply that this bound is tight.}
elements.sent-2411-061: In our amortized analysis we exploit the close relationship between weight-biased leftist heaps and top-down skew heaps.
elements.sent-2411-062: We define potential function $\Pot{}$ as \begin{equation}{lll} \Pot{\Bin{}} = 0, \Pot{\Bin{t,a,u}} = \Pot{(t)} + \pot{t}{u} + \Pot{(u)}, \end{equation} where \begin{equation}\pot{t}{u} = \max\left(\log_\beta \frac{\beta\sz{u}}{\sz{t}+\sz{u}}, 0\right),\end{equation} with $\beta=\phi^{\phi+2}\approx 5.703$, following~\cite{KS91 ,~\cite[Lemma~9.2]{Sch92 .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The essential property that we will need for this potential function is that \begin{equation}\label{eq:phi-prop} \sz{t}\leq\sz{u} \Rightarrow \pot{u}{t}\leq\pot{t}{u},\end{equation} as $\log_\beta$ is increasing.
elements.sent-2411-065: The central operation to analyze is \ch{x {y .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Because of the balancing step we cannot reuse the analysis of~\cite{KS91 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Instead we will directly prove by induction on $x$ and $y$ that \begin{equation}\label{eq:indhyp} \A{\ch{}{}}{x,y} \leq \log_\alpha \sz{\ch{x}{y}} + \log_\beta \sz{x} + \log_\beta \sz{y},\end{equation} with $\alpha=\phi^{2\phi-1}\approx 2.933$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Then $\ch{x {y =\bal{\Bin{t,a,\ch{u {y }$ and \begin{equation}{lll} \A{\ch{}{}}{x,y} = \T{\ch{}{}}{x,y} + \Pot{(\ch{x}{y})} - \Pot{(x)} - \Pot{(y)} [1mm] = 1 + \T{\ch{}{}}{u,y} + \Pot{(\rm bal \Bin{t,a,\ch{u}{y}})} -\Pot{\Bin{t,a,u}} - \Pot{(y)} [1mm] = \A{\ch{}{}}{u,y} + 1 - \pot{t}{u} + \left\{{ll} \pot{t}{\ch{u}{y}} , if \sz{t}>\sz{\ch{u}{y}} \pot{\ch{u}{y}}{t} , if \sz{t}\leq\sz{\ch{u}{y}} \right. [1mm] \leq \A{\ch{}{}}{u,y} + 1 - \pot{t}{u} + \pot{\ch{u}{y}}{t} [1mm] \leq \log_\alpha \sz{\ch{u}{y}} +\pot{\ch{u}{y}}{t} + \log_\beta \sz{u} + 1 - \pot{t}{u} + \log_\beta \sz{y} [1mm] \leq \log_\alpha \sz{\ch{u}{y}} +\pot{\ch{u}{y}}{t} + \log_\beta (\sz{t} +\sz{u}) + \log_\beta \sz{y} [1mm] \leq \log_\alpha (\sz{t} + \sz{\ch{u}{y}}) + \log_\beta \sz{x} + \log_\beta \sz{y} [1mm] = \log_\alpha \sz{\ch{x}{y}} + \log_\beta \sz{x} + \log_\beta \sz{y}, \end{equation} where the first inequality follows from (\ref{eq:phi-prop ), the second inequality follows from the induction hypothesis, the third inequality follows from the definition of $\varphi$, and the last inequality follows as it was shown in~\cite{KS91 that \begin{equation}\log_\beta \frac{\beta n}{m+n} \leq \log_\alpha \frac{m+n}{m},\end{equation} for all positive $m$ and $n$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: With both \sz{x and \sz{y bounded by \sz{\ch{x {y , we conclude from (\ref{eq:indhyp ) that \begin{equation}\A{\ch{}{}}{x,y} \leq \log_\phi \sz{\ch{x}{y}},\end{equation} as $\log_\alpha \phi + 2\log_\beta \phi = 1/(2\phi-1) + 2/(\phi+2) = 1$ (using $\phi^2=\phi+1$).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: With this we have proved our first main result.

elements.section-2411-005: Amortized Analysis of Rank-Biased Leftist Heaps
elements.sent-2411-076: For rank-biased heaps we do not have a similar improvement over the bounds from Section~\ref{section:simple-bounds .}

elements.sent-2411-102: In this section we show that the worst-case sequences of operations on skew heaps from \cite{Sch97tight also apply to weight-biased leftist heaps and rank-biased leftist heaps.}

elements.sent-2411-104: We let $\l{n}$ denote Hofstadter's G-sequence \cite{Hof79 defined by $\l{0 =0$ and $\l{n =n-\l{\l{n-1 }$, $n\geq1$, see also \url{www.oeis.org/A005206 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: A golden tree $\b{n}$ of order $n$, $n\geq0$, is defined recursively as the following unlabeled binary tree: \begin{equation}{lcll} \b{0} = \Bin{}, \b{n} = \Bin{\b{\l{n-1}},\b{\r{n-1}}}, n\geq1. \end{equation} The golden trees form a supersequence of the (unlabeled) Fibonacci trees~\cite[Section~6.2.1]{Knu98 in the sense that a golden tree $\b{n}$ corresponds to the Fibonacci tree of order $k$ whenever $n=\f{k+2 -1$, $k\geq0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: See Figure~\ref{fig , trees $\b{0 , \b{1 , \b{2 , \b{4 , \b{7}$, and $\b{12}$ correspond to Fibonacci trees.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Clearly, golden trees are weight-biased leftist trees as $\size{G_n =n$ and $\l{n}\geq\r{n}$ \cite[Lemma~3]{Sch97tight .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In fact, $\l{n /\r{n}$ approaches the golden ratio $\phi$ for larger $n$, which explains the name for these trees.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Since $R(n)$ is nondecreasing \cite[Lemma~3]{Sch97tight , it is easily seen that $\rank{\b{n }$ is also nondecreasing in $n$, which implies that golden trees are also rank-biased leftist trees.}}

elements.sent-2411-114: This ``self-recreating'' property is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig2 , where operation \ch{x {y is split into two stages.}}}

elements.sent-2411-117: With this we have proved our second main result.
elements.sent-2411-118: We have analyzed the amortized complexity of leftist heaps.
elements.sent-2411-119: Compared to the well-known worst-case bounds, our amortized bounds provide a more refined view of the computational complexity of leftist heaps.
elements.sent-2411-120: Another example of such a refined amortized analysis is the analysis of insertions into AVL-trees by Mehlhorn and Tsakalidis~\cite{MT86 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: AVL-trees are height-balanced and achieve logarithmic time for the main operations in the worst case.
elements.sent-2411-122: The amortized bounds yield a more profound understanding of the time complexity and may also help in experimental studies of these data structures, e.g., to understand why skew heaps and leftist heaps behave more similarly than one might anticipate.
elements.sent-2411-123: For weight-biased heaps, we have been able to reduce the bound for \DLM{x from $2\,\log_2 \sz{x}$ to $\log_\phi \sz{x}\approx 1.44\,\log_2 \sz{x}$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: We would like to point out that the improved bounds from~\cite{KS91 are essential for this result.}
elements.sent-2411-125: For the potential function $\Pot{}$ from~\cite{ST86 , with $\pot{t {u =1$ if $\sz{t < \sz{u}$ and $\pot{t {u =0$ otherwise, we do not get an improvement over the worst-case bounds.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Indeed, for any weight-biased heap $x$ we would simply have $\Pot{(x) =0$.}
elements.sent-2411-127: For rank-biased heaps, all potential functions considered in this paper (including the above potential function from~\cite{ST86 ) fall short of achieving any improvement over the worst-case bound for \DLM{x .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: As explained in Section~\ref{section:amor-rank , this leaves us with an interesting open problem regarding the computational complexity of rank-biased leftist heaps: whether the amortized cost of \DLM{x is equal to $\log_\phi \sz{x}$, equal to $2\,\log_2 \sz{x}$, or something in between.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Hopefully, innovations such as ATLAS~\cite{LMZ21 will help us in cracking this problem, expediting the search for potential functions beating the worst-case bound for \DLM{ .}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Moreover, as another interesting direction for further research (also in light of~\cite{BKK+23 ), we introduce the {\em randomized leftist heaps.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: This probabilistic variant is defined as in Figure~\ref{tdsh , but with the following balancing strategy: \begin{equation}{lcll} \bal{\Bin{t,a,u}} = \Bin{t,a,u}, \ \ with probability $1-p$, \Bar \Bin{u,a,t}, \ \ with probability $p$, \end{equation} where $0\leq p\leq1$ is a fixed bias.}
elements.sent-2411-132: For $p=1/2$, the behavior of these heaps resembles the randomized meldable heaps of~\cite{GM98 , for which the expected costs of \UNI{x {y and \DLM{x are bounded by $2\log_2\sz{\ch{x {y }$ and $2\log_2\sz{x}$, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Note that these bounds coincide with the worst-case bounds for leftists heaps.
elements.sent-2411-134: For $p=1$, however, the randomized leftist heaps reduce to skew heaps, for which we have superior amortized bounds.
elements.sent-2411-135: Thus, the question is which value for $p$ will be optimal in which sense, e.g., minimizing the expected amortized costs {\em and ensuring that the worst-case actual costs are also limited with high probability (e.g., what about $p=1/\phi$).}
elements.sent-2411-136: The results for randomized splay trees (see~\cite{LMZ22 and references therein) seem a good starting point for such an amortized analysis.. {\bf Acknowledgments.}}
